reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The digital-economy/labour-market interest prioritises two documented values: (1) reducing preparation-sensitive barriers to talent identification so that high-ability pupils from all SES backgrounds can be detected and developed early; (2) maintaining institutional stability and predictability in school structures so that employer partnerships and curriculum-to-employment pipelines remain administratively viable. S1 supports value (1) but introduces risk to value (2) via the untested SES-proxy mechanism and cross-maintainer coordination challenges.
reactions.interest: Employers and tech-sector stakeholders who rely on selective-track schools as a pipeline for high-ability early talent identification and intensive development.
reactions.public_good_frame: A labour market that draws talent from across the SES spectrum, not just high-SES families, increases the quality and diversity of the skilled workforce, reducing human-capital waste and improving competitiveness.
reactions.argument: S1's stated goal — reweighting entry probabilities toward lower-SES applicants at a given ability level via a less-coaching-sensitive assessment — directly serves the digital-economy interest in broadening talent identification. However, three conditions undermine conditional support: (a) the SES-proxy assumption is flagged as 'low confidence' because Hungary lacks an administrative student-level SES register; (b) the 'coaching-resistant assessment' is unvalidated in Hungary, creating risk that a new instrument either proves vulnerable to the same preparation arms-race or fails to predict academic/employment outcomes (and thus loses its value as a talent-sorting tool); (c) cross-maintainer compliance is treated as weak evidence—if church and foundation schools opt out or set parallel admission criteria, the reform fragments into multiple signals, reducing clarity for employers tracking school-quality cohorts. The digital-economy sector benefits from one clear, stable, nationwide talent-pipeline. Fragmentation is costly. That said, if the SES-proxy can be solved and the assessment genuinely resists coaching while maintaining predictive validity, the reform would strengthen the pipeline by surfacing high-ability low-SES pupils who currently self-select out.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes full if: (i) a feasibility study (KSH/Oktatási Hivatal linkage of administrative data) demonstrates a workable, gaming-resistant SES proxy is achievable within 2 years; (ii) piloting of alternative assessment formats against coaching-intervention groups shows the new format maintains or improves predictive validity for later academic/labour-market outcomes; (iii) church and foundation maintainers commit to uniform admission-equity rules in writing, or the legislature enacts a binding amendment to the Nkt. Before those conditions are met, the risk of implementation failure or fragmentation is too high for employers to rely on stable talent signalling. Opposition emerges if: the SES proxy is abandoned in favour of unweighted lottery-among-qualified, which would reduce the predictive validity of selective-place admission for employer recruitment; or if the new assessment is shown to correlate worse with later achievement/employment outcomes than the current exam.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The digital-economy interest depends on a sufficient volume of deeply-developed high-ability talent in a concentrated, recognizable institutional pipeline. S2's gradual phase-down of selective seats directly reduces that volume without demonstrably offsetting it through improved peer composition in general schools (the 'contested peer-effect mechanism'). The interest tolerates structural consolidation (S3) because it replaces one pipeline with another (comprehensive schools with strong diagnostic/remedial capacity). It opposes S2 because it shrinks the pipeline without replacement.
reactions.interest: Employers and tech-sector stakeholders who depend on selective-track schools as a high-volume, concentrated talent source for early identification and intensive secondary education.
reactions.public_good_frame: A narrowing selective sector may reduce the overall stock of deeply-developed high-ability talent entering the labour market, lowering skilled-workforce supply and competitiveness, unless offset by superior talent development elsewhere (which S2 does not show).
reactions.argument: S2's mechanism relies on the claim that 'fewer selective seats keep more high-achievers in the common track, which can improve peer composition in urban general-school upper grades.' This mechanism is flagged by the scenario itself as 'contested' and 'weak/contested' by equity_and_social_mobility and devil_advocate. For the digital-economy interest, this is disqualifying: if high-achievers remain in general schools, the intensity, duration, and depth of their academic development is empirically lower than in selective tracks (per the hungarian_education_system expert's characterization of track differentiation). A weak peer-effect mechanism does not compensate for the loss of intensive selective-track development. Moreover, S2 explicitly assumes that 'caps on state-maintained schools are not simply offset by non-state maintainers expanding selective intake'—but this assumption is 'directly contradicted by school_network_planning's finding that non-state maintainers can absorb selective tracks and bypass consolidation plans.' If non-state (church, foundation) sectors expand to capture displaced demand, the volume loss may be nominal, but the fragmentation of the pipeline (some talent now in private/foundation schools outside standard employer recruitment channels) still reduces clarity and accessibility for employer talent identification. Demographic decline provides a framing cover, but it does not solve the underlying problem: fewer recognizable, concentrated, high-intensity talent-development seats. For a labour market dependent on consistent early talent signalling, S2 is a net loss.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition softens to 'conditional' if: (i) a longitudinal peer-effect study in Hungarian primary schools following selection events demonstrates that high-achievers retained in general schools experience demonstrably equivalent academic development intensity (measured by time-on-task, curriculum depth, qualified-teacher allocation) as selective-track peers; (ii) church and foundation maintainers commit in writing not to expand selective intake as state-sector caps tighten, or the legislature enacts binding caps across all maintainers; (iii) a replacement talent-identification and development mechanism for general-school high-achievers is defined and piloted (e.g., universal gifted-education diagnostics in year 6-7, with guaranteed advanced placement for identified pupils). Opposition becomes support only if all three conditions are met and the peer-effect study shows effect sizes comparable to selective-track development gains (i.e., the trade-off is not merely reduced sorting but maintained talent depth).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The digital-economy interest prioritises talent identification and development intensity. S3 abolishes early sorting but promises to rebuild diagnostic and remedial capacity at a later stage (age 14+) and to upgrade teacher training to handle heterogeneous classrooms. If the Finnish-style bundled investment is delivered at promised scale and quality, this could maintain talent development intensity while widening the pool from which talent is identified (by delaying sorting and removing SES-coaching advantage at entry). The condition is credible implementation of the bundled investment—which is where confidence is low.
reactions.interest: Employers and tech-sector stakeholders who need reliably identified and intensively developed high-ability talent, and who accept structural reform if an alternative talent-development infrastructure is built at equivalent depth.
reactions.public_good_frame: A comprehensive lower-secondary system with robust early diagnostic and remedial support can identify high-ability pupils from all SES backgrounds at age 14+ without the sunk cost of SES-biased sorting at age 10/12, and can retain that talent at higher intensity if teacher quality and class sizes support heterogeneous instruction. This expands the talent pool and increases labour-market skill supply.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that attempts to replace the sorting mechanism rather than shrink or reweight it. The digital-economy interest can accept this if the replacement infrastructure is built. The scenario explicitly notes that 'bundling the reform with upgraded teacher training and early diagnostic/remedial support is necessary for equity gains to materialise, per the Finnish precedent.' This is also necessary for the labour-market interest to be served: without upgraded diagnostics and teacher quality, the delay in sorting is merely a delay in identifying talent, not a net gain in talent development. However, three conditions create conditional rather than full support: (a) the scenario flags that 'teacher supply and quality in comprehensive schools can be upgraded fast enough' is an assumption 'contradicted by implementation_planning's finding that Hungary's teacher shortage is 'binding' and likely to worsen.' If teacher shortages worsen under S3, the heterogeneous-classroom instruction that is supposed to retain high-achievers will fail, and talent development intensity will decline. (b) The scenario itself identifies 'the largest risk' as 'middle-class exit to non-state or private schools, or to general-school choice/catchment sorting,' which would relocate rather than reduce sorting—and if high-ability pupils exit to non-standard schools, the employer pipeline becomes fragmented and unreliable. (c) Political durability is flagged as 'low confidence' given the Polish reversal precedent (1999-2016/19); if S3 is reversed after 4-6 years due to political pressure, Hungary will have disrupted the selective-track pipeline without having established a replacement, leaving a talent-identification gap. That said, if the three binding conditions are met—(i) teacher supply is sustainably upgraded through salary incentives and retraining, (ii) middle-class exit is monitored and policy levers are deployed to prevent fragmentation, (iii) multi-party political commitment survives two electoral cycles—then S3 offers the digital-economy interest a structurally superior outcome: a wider, less SES-biased talent pool identified and developed at equivalent depth.
reactions.condition_to_change: Conditional support becomes full if: (i) the Ministry of Education secures and publicly commits to a decade-long, dedicated funding stream for teacher retraining and early diagnostic/remedial support (Finnish-style bundling), with quarterly oversight reporting; (ii) workforce exit tracking and comprehensive teacher union survey data show that teacher supply is not declining but stabilising or improving under the reform; (iii) matched-pair school comparisons pre/post reform show that SES composition across all maintainer types is narrowing (not widening), indicating that sorting is being reduced rather than relocated; (iv) a multi-party political pact is signed with explicit provisions for locking the reform beyond single electoral cycles, and polling data across the implementation period shows sustained public support. Conditional support becomes opposition if: (a) teacher shortages visibly worsen after year 2 of implementation, or (b) middle-class exit to non-state/private schools accelerates to >15% of the cohort, or (c) the political durability condition fails (i.e., reversal pressures mount within 4 years of implementation), indicating that the replacement infrastructure will not be completed.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: The digital-economy interest depends on sustained, intensive talent development in a recognizable institutional pipeline. S4 leaves the sorting mechanism intact and attempts to 'compensate' downstream through targeted funding and curriculum standards for general schools. However, compensation does not replace sorting-driven talent identification; it only masks its effects. For the labour-market interest, this is a net loss because (a) it preserves the SES bias in who enters the selective-track talent pipeline, (b) it does not build diagnostic/remedial capacity to identify high-ability low-SES pupils in general schools, and (c) it entrenches rather than disrupts the sorting engine, creating permanent structural dependency on compensation budgets that are empirically vulnerable to fiscal consolidation.
reactions.interest: Employers and tech-sector stakeholders who depend on stable, high-volume, high-intensity talent identification and development, and who require predictable, durable institutional infrastructure for employer-school partnerships.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation-without-structural-change locks Hungary into permanent underutilisation of high-ability talent from lower-SES backgrounds, reducing the overall stock of skilled labour available to employers and impairing competitiveness. Durability risk (budget vulnerability) makes S4 an unstable policy foundation for long-term labour-market planning.
reactions.argument: S4 is explicitly described by the scenario itself as addressing sorting effects 'downstream' while leaving 'the sorting mechanism itself intact.' For the digital-economy interest, this is categorically insufficient. The mechanism by which selective-track entry at age 10 produces SES bias operates at the point of entry, not in the downstream instruction of those already sorted. Targeted funding and curriculum standards may narrow outcome gaps within the general-school majority, but they do not address the root problem: high-ability low-SES pupils do not apply to selective tracks (or are filtered out by preparation requirements), and therefore never enter the intensive-development pipeline at all. S4 treats this as acceptable if general-school instruction is improved enough. But the scenario's own equity_impact statement notes that 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age (10-12).' This is directly relevant to the labour-market interest: if no country has shown that downstream compensation can offset the SES-gradient effects of sorting at age 10, why should the digital-economy sector expect it to work in Hungary? Moreover, the implementation risks are severe: (a) 'cost_checker flags targeted funding for disadvantaged schools as the first line likely to be cut in fiscal consolidation'—meaning S4's durability is weakest precisely when economic downturns most damage the pipeline; (b) 'implementation_planning documents a binding Hungarian teacher shortage,' which means that curriculum-standards improvements and grade-retention-reduction support programmes depend on teacher supply that is already critical; (c) the scenario notes that compensation 'may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine, foreclosing future structural reform options,' meaning S4 trades near-term political ease for permanent structural lock-in of inequitable talent identification. For an interest dependent on stable, long-term institutional infrastructure, S4 offers the worst trade: it preserves SES-biased sorting, provides no alternative talent-identification mechanism for disadvantaged pupils, and creates fiscal/political lock-in that precludes reform once the compensation commitment becomes politically difficult. S4 is the scenario most likely to result in permanent talent waste.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition would soften to 'no_position' (i.e., the labour-market interest would be indifferent) only if: (i) a decade-long Hungarian pilot with matched comparison districts demonstrates that SES-gradient narrowing under S4 reaches effect sizes equivalent to or exceeding those documented in S3 comprehensive-system comparisons (i.e., compensation genuinely offsets rather than masks sorting effects); (ii) the Ministry of Finance publishes a binding, multi-year (10-year minimum) budgetary commitment to TEIP-equivalent funding, with automatic escalators tied to education inflation and a statutory bar against consolidation-era cuts; (iii) teacher supply and qualification data show that Hungary's teacher shortage is not binding (i.e., there is capacity for the grade-retention and remedial-support programmes to be delivered at intended quality). Until these conditions are met, the labour-market interest cannot rely on S4 as a durable solution. Opposition becomes support only if all three conditions are demonstrably met AND the pilot data show that SES-gradient narrowing is sustained over a decade—a threshold S4 is not positioned to achieve.
